---
title: "Fluoroquinolones"
format:
  revealjs:
    self-contained: true
    spotlight: false
    touch: true
    controls: true
    pointer: true
    center-title-slide: false
    transition-speed: fast
    scrollable: true
    slide-number: true
    incremental: false
    multiplex: false
    chalkboard: false
    theme: [custom.scss]
bibliography: fluoroquinolone-references.bib
lightbox: true
csl: diagnostic-microbiology-and-infectious-disease.csl
---

## Fluoroquinolones {background-color="#b20e10" background-video="fluoroquinolone-images/ecoli.mp4" background-video-loop="true" background-video-muted="true" background-opacity="0.4"}

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

<center>

**Russell E. Lewis** <br> Associate Professor of Infectious Diseases (MEDS-10/B) <br>

<br> <br>

![](fluoroquinolone-images/DMM_newlogo.png){fig-align="center" width="350"}

<br> {{< fa solid envelope size=1x >}} russelledward.lewis\@unipd.it <br> {{< fa brands github size=1x >}} [https://github.com/Russlewisbo](https://github.com/Russlewisbo/ESCMID_2022_talk) <br> Slides and course materials: [www.idpadova.com](https://padovaid.com/)

</center>

# Introduction & Overview

## Learning Objectives {.smaller}

By the end of this lecture, you should be able to:

::: {.incremental}
1. Describe the mechanism of action of fluoroquinolones
2. Explain resistance mechanisms and their clinical implications
3. Compare the spectrum of activity among different quinolones
4. Apply pharmacokinetic principles to dosing decisions
5. Select appropriate quinolone therapy for common infections
6. Recognize important adverse effects and drug interactions
:::

::: {.notes}
Welcome to our lecture on quinolones. This is a comprehensive class of antibiotics that has evolved significantly since nalidixic acid was first introduced in the 1960s. Today we'll cover everything from mechanism to clinical application.
:::

## Quinolone Evolution {.smaller}

| Generation | Examples | Key Features |
|------------|----------|--------------|
| 1st | Nalidixic acid | UTI only; narrow spectrum |
| 2nd | Norfloxacin, Ciprofloxacin | Broader gram-negative; systemic use |
| 3rd | Levofloxacin | Enhanced gram-positive |
| 4th | Moxifloxacin, Gemifloxacin | Enhanced respiratory pathogens |

::: {.notes}
The quinolones have evolved dramatically. First-generation agents like nalidixic acid were limited to UTIs. Modern fluoroquinolones have much broader activity and systemic applications.
:::

## Currently Available Quinolones

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Older Agents:**

- Norfloxacin
- Ciprofloxacin
- Ofloxacin
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Newer Agents:**

- Levofloxacin
- Moxifloxacin
- Gemifloxacin
- Delafloxacin
:::
::::

::: {.notes}
These are the quinolones currently available in clinical practice. Ciprofloxacin and levofloxacin are the most commonly used. Delafloxacin is the newest addition, approved for skin infections.
:::

# Mechanism of Action

## Primary Targets: Topoisomerases

Quinolones target two essential bacterial enzymes:

::: {.incremental}
- **DNA Gyrase** (GyrA, GyrB subunits)
- **Topoisomerase IV** (ParC, ParE subunits)
:::

Both are Type II topoisomerases required for DNA replication.

::: {.notes}
The key to understanding quinolone action is recognizing these two targets. Both are essential for bacterial survival, and quinolones exploit this by trapping them in complexes with DNA.
:::

## DNA Gyrase Function

DNA gyrase uniquely:

::: {.incremental}
- Introduces negative superhelical twists into DNA
- Removes positive supertwists ahead of replication fork
- Regulates DNA superhelicity (with Topoisomerase I)
:::

> Encoded by *gyrA* and *gyrB* genes

::: {.notes}
DNA gyrase is the only enzyme that can introduce negative supercoils. This is essential for compacting the bacterial chromosome and for DNA replication to proceed.
:::

## Topoisomerase IV Function

Topoisomerase IV:

::: {.incremental}
- Decatenates (resolves) interlinked daughter DNA molecules
- Allows DNA segregation into daughter cells
- Cannot introduce negative supercoils
:::

> Encoded by *parC* and *parE* genes

::: {.notes}
When circular DNA replicates, the two daughter molecules are interlocked like chain links. Topoisomerase IV separates them so they can partition into daughter cells.
:::

## Species-Dependent Primary Targets

| Organism Type | Primary Target | Secondary Target |
|---------------|----------------|------------------|
| Gram-negative | DNA Gyrase | Topoisomerase IV |
| Gram-positive | Topoisomerase IV | DNA Gyrase |

::: {.notes}
This is clinically important because it affects resistance development. The organism's more sensitive enzyme determines the primary target for a given quinolone.
:::

## Mechanism of Bacterial Killing {.smaller}

How quinolones kill bacteria:

::: {.incremental}
1. Bind to topoisomerase-DNA complex (not enzyme alone)
2. Block resealing of DNA double-strand breaks
3. Stabilize covalent enzyme-DNA intermediates
4. Create barriers to replication fork and transcription
5. Convert to permanent DNA breaks → cell death
:::

::: {.notes}
Quinolones don't simply inhibit enzyme function—they convert these enzymes into DNA-damaging agents. This "poisoning" of the topoisomerases is what makes quinolones bactericidal.
:::

## Bactericidal Activity Features

::: {.callout-important}
## Key Point
- Bactericidal activity increases with drug concentration
- Maximum killing at ~30× MIC
- Paradoxical decrease at very high concentrations
:::

**Post-antibiotic effect:** 1-2 hours

::: {.notes}
The concentration-dependent killing and post-antibiotic effect support once-daily or extended-interval dosing strategies that maximize peak concentrations.
:::

# Resistance Mechanisms

## Overview of Resistance

Quinolone resistance occurs through:

::: {.incremental}
1. **Target mutations** — alterations in GyrA, GyrB, ParC, ParE
2. **Efflux pumps** — active drug extrusion
3. **Plasmid-mediated** — qnr genes, modifying enzymes
:::

::: {.notes}
Resistance is multifactorial and often involves combinations of these mechanisms. Understanding them helps predict cross-resistance patterns and guide empirical therapy.
:::

## Target Mutations: QRDR

**Quinolone Resistance-Determining Region:**

- GyrA mutations cluster at amino acids 67-106
- Most common: Serine-83 → Leucine/Tryptophan
- Similar "hot spots" in ParC (position 80)

::: {.notes}
The QRDR is near the active site of the enzyme. Mutations here reduce quinolone binding while preserving enzyme function. Serine-83 mutations in GyrA cause the largest resistance increments.
:::

## Stepwise Resistance Development

::: {.incremental}
1. First mutation in primary target enzyme
2. Low-level resistance (8-fold MIC increase)
3. Second mutation in secondary target
4. High-level clinical resistance
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
Serial passage selects progressively resistant mutants!
:::

::: {.notes}
This is why monotherapy for serious infections can be problematic. Sequential mutations accumulate, and having two targets provides only partial protection if both can mutate.
:::

## Efflux Pumps: Gram-Negatives {.smaller}

| Organism | Pump System | Clinical Significance |
|----------|-------------|----------------------|
| *E. coli* | AcrAB-TolC | Often combined with gyrA mutations |
| *P. aeruginosa* | MexAB-OprM | Common in clinical isolates |
| *P. aeruginosa* | MexCD-OprJ | Selected by fluoroquinolone use |
| *K. pneumoniae* | OqxAB-TolC | Plasmid-encoded |

::: {.notes}
These pumps belong to the RND family and have broad substrate profiles—hence the term "multidrug resistance pumps." Overexpression contributes to resistance to multiple antibiotic classes.
:::

## Efflux Pumps: Gram-Positives

**S. aureus:**

- NorA — hydrophilic quinolones (norfloxacin > ciprofloxacin > levofloxacin)
- NorB, NorC — broader substrate range
- MepA — MATE family pump

::: {.callout-tip}
Moxifloxacin is not affected by NorA overexpression!
:::

::: {.notes}
Efflux pumps in gram-positives show variable substrate profiles. This explains why some quinolones retain activity when others don't, even against the same organism.
:::

## Plasmid-Mediated Resistance: Qnr {.smaller}

**Qnr proteins** protect topoisomerases from quinolone action:

- Seven families: qnrA, qnrB, qnrS, qnrC, qnrD, qnrE, qnrVC
- Found in 1-7% of Enterobacterales
- Alone cause only low-level resistance
- **BUT:** Facilitate selection of chromosomal mutations

::: {.notes}
Qnr genes are particularly concerning because they lower the threshold for selecting resistant mutants. They're often found on plasmids carrying other resistance genes, contributing to MDR.
:::

## Plasmid-Mediated: Modifying Enzymes

**AAC(6')-Ib-cr:**

::: {.incremental}
- Variant aminoglycoside acetyltransferase
- Acetylates piperazinyl nitrogen of ciprofloxacin/norfloxacin
- Causes 3-4 fold MIC increase
- Does NOT affect moxifloxacin, levofloxacin
:::

::: {.notes}
This enzyme specifically targets quinolones with an unsubstituted piperazinyl nitrogen. It's another example of how resistance mechanisms can show quinolone-specific effects.
:::

## Clinical Implications of Resistance

::: {.callout-warning}
## Rising Resistance
- *E. coli*: 20-40% resistance in some areas
- *P. aeruginosa*: 20-30% resistance
- *N. gonorrhoeae*: No longer recommended empirically
- *Salmonella*: Increasing worldwide
:::

::: {.notes}
These resistance rates have major clinical implications. We can no longer rely on fluoroquinolones for empirical treatment of gonorrhea, and their use for other infections requires local susceptibility data.
:::

# Antimicrobial Activity

## Spectrum Overview

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Best Activity:**

- Enterobacterales
- *Haemophilus* spp.
- *Neisseria* spp.
- *Moraxella catarrhalis*
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Variable Activity:**

- *P. aeruginosa* (cipro, levo)
- Streptococci (newer agents)
- Anaerobes (moxi, dela)
:::
::::

::: {.notes}
Understanding the spectrum helps guide agent selection. Ciprofloxacin remains the most potent agent against gram-negatives, while newer agents have broader gram-positive coverage.
:::

## Gram-Negative Activity

::: {.callout-tip}
## Ciprofloxacin
Remains the most potent quinolone against gram-negative bacteria, especially *P. aeruginosa*
:::

- Only ciprofloxacin and levofloxacin suitable for *Pseudomonas*
- Resistance may emerge during monotherapy for serious infections

::: {.notes}
For serious pseudomonal infections, combination therapy is often recommended to prevent resistance emergence. Ciprofloxacin's gram-negative potency makes it the preferred quinolone for these infections.
:::

## Gram-Positive Activity Comparison {.smaller}

| Organism | Cipro | Levo | Moxi | Gemi | Dela |
|----------|-------|------|------|------|------|
| MSSA | 0.5 | 0.25 | 0.12 | 0.06 | 0.008 |
| MRSA | ≥32 | 16 | 4 | 8 | 0.5 |
| *S. pneumoniae* | 2 | 1 | 0.25 | 0.06 | 0.015 |

MIC~90~ values (μg/mL)

::: {.notes}
Note how delafloxacin maintains good activity even against MRSA. This explains its niche for skin infections where staphylococci are common. Moxifloxacin and gemifloxacin are preferred for pneumococcal infections.
:::

## Respiratory Pathogens

"Respiratory fluoroquinolones" have enhanced activity against:

::: {.incremental}
- *Streptococcus pneumoniae*
- *Legionella pneumophila*
- *Mycoplasma pneumoniae*
- *Chlamydia pneumoniae*
:::

**Agents:** Levofloxacin, Moxifloxacin, Gemifloxacin

::: {.notes}
This enhanced respiratory pathogen coverage makes these agents attractive for CAP monotherapy, covering both typical and atypical pathogens with a single agent.
:::

## Anaerobic Activity

| Agent | B. fragilis MIC~90~ |
|-------|---------------------|
| Ciprofloxacin | 4-64 |
| Levofloxacin | 2->16 |
| **Moxifloxacin** | **0.5-8** |
| **Delafloxacin** | **0.12** |

::: {.callout-note}
Most quinolones have poor anaerobic activity; moxifloxacin and delafloxacin are exceptions.
:::

::: {.notes}
This is important for intra-abdominal infections and diabetic foot infections where anaerobic coverage is needed. Ciprofloxacin requires additional anaerobic coverage for these indications.
:::

## Mycobacterial Activity {.smaller}

| Agent | M. tuberculosis | M. avium | M. fortuitum |
|-------|-----------------|----------|--------------|
| Ciprofloxacin | 1 | 16 | 0.3->4 |
| Levofloxacin | 0.25-1 | 0.5-64 | 0.06-2 |
| Moxifloxacin | 0.125-0.5 | 0.5-16 | 0.06-1 |

::: {.notes}
Moxifloxacin is the most potent agent against M. tuberculosis and is now included in MDR-TB regimens. Activity against MAC is poor across all agents.
:::

## Anthrax and Bioterrorism

::: {.callout-important}
## CDC Recommendations
- Ciprofloxacin and levofloxacin recommended for anthrax prophylaxis
- Similar efficacy to doxycycline
- Quinolone resistance can be selected in *B. anthracis*
:::

::: {.notes}
In a bioterrorism scenario, resistance cannot be assumed. The concern is that an engineered resistant strain could be used, making empirical quinolone prophylaxis ineffective.
:::

# Pharmacology

## Absorption Characteristics

::: {.incremental}
- Bioavailability: 50-100% (most >70%)
- Peak levels: 1-3 hours post-dose
- Food delays but doesn't significantly reduce absorption
- Enteral feeding may reduce absorption
:::

::: {.notes}
The excellent oral bioavailability of most quinolones allows oral step-down therapy and outpatient treatment of serious infections. This is a major advantage over many other antibiotic classes.
:::

## Peak Serum Concentrations {.smaller}

| Quinolone | Dose (mg) | C~max~ (μg/mL) |
|-----------|-----------|----------------|
| Norfloxacin | 400 PO | 1.5 |
| Ciprofloxacin | 500 PO | 2.4 |
| Ofloxacin | 400 PO | 4.6 |
| Levofloxacin | 500 PO | 5.7 |
| Moxifloxacin | 400 PO | 4.3 |
| Delafloxacin | 450 PO | 7.45 |

::: {.notes}
Delafloxacin achieves the highest peak levels. For concentration-dependent killing, these peak levels matter for efficacy and reducing resistance selection.
:::

## Tissue Distribution {.smaller}

Concentrations **exceeding** serum levels:

| Site | Fold Increase |
|------|---------------|
| Feces | 100-1000× |
| Macrophages/Neutrophils | 2->100× |
| Bile | 2-20× |
| Lung tissue | 1.6-6× |
| Prostate tissue | 0.9-2.3× |

::: {.notes}
High intracellular concentrations make quinolones effective against intracellular pathogens like Legionella, Chlamydia, and mycobacteria. Prostate penetration supports use in prostatitis.
:::

## CSF Penetration

::: {.callout-warning}
CSF penetration is generally **LOW** without meningeal inflammation
:::

- Better than β-lactams in uninflamed meninges
- Levofloxacin ≈ Moxifloxacin > Ciprofloxacin
- Active efflux may limit CNS concentrations

::: {.notes}
While quinolones are not first-line for meningitis, they may have a role in specific situations like tuberculous meningitis or when other options are limited.
:::

## Elimination Routes

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Primarily Renal:**

- Ofloxacin
- Levofloxacin

Requires dose adjustment in renal impairment
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Primarily Hepatic:**

- Moxifloxacin
- Nalidixic acid

No renal dose adjustment
:::
::::

::: {.notes}
Knowing the elimination route is essential for dosing. Moxifloxacin is particularly useful when you don't want to adjust for renal function or when high urinary concentrations aren't needed.
:::

## Renal Dosing Adjustments {.smaller}

| Agent | CrCl 10-50 | CrCl <10 |
|-------|------------|----------|
| Norfloxacin | q24h | q24h |
| Ciprofloxacin | q18h | q24h |
| Ofloxacin | q24h | ½ dose q24h |
| Levofloxacin | ½ dose q24h | ½ dose q48h |
| **Moxifloxacin** | **No change** | **No change** |

::: {.notes}
Moxifloxacin's lack of need for renal adjustment makes it attractive in patients with fluctuating renal function or when levels won't be monitored closely.
:::

## Drug Interaction: Cations

::: {.callout-important}
## Critical Interaction
Aluminum, magnesium, calcium, iron, and zinc form poorly absorbed chelates with quinolones.

**Timing:** Take quinolone 2 hours before or 2-6 hours after these agents
:::

::: {.notes}
This is one of the most important drug interactions to remember. Antacids, supplements, sucralfate, and even tube feeds can dramatically reduce quinolone absorption.
:::

## Drug Interaction: CYP1A2 {.smaller}

**Ciprofloxacin inhibits CYP1A2:**

| Drug | Effect |
|------|--------|
| Theophylline | 30% ↓ clearance; monitor levels |
| Caffeine | ↑ levels |
| Tizanidine | ↑ CNS/hypotensive effects; AVOID |
| Clozapine | ↑ levels; monitor |

::: {.callout-note}
Other quinolones have minimal CYP1A2 effects
:::

::: {.notes}
Ciprofloxacin is the main culprit here. The tizanidine interaction is particularly important—concomitant use can cause severe hypotension and sedation.
:::

## Drug Interaction: QT Prolongation

::: {.callout-warning}
Avoid combining with other QT-prolonging agents:

- Antiarrhythmics (Class IA, III)
- Antipsychotics
- TCAs
- Macrolides
- Azole antifungals
:::

**Risk:** Moxifloxacin > Levofloxacin > Ciprofloxacin

::: {.notes}
QT prolongation is a class effect but varies by agent. In high-risk patients, consider ciprofloxacin if a quinolone is necessary.
:::

# Clinical Applications

## Urinary Tract Infections

**Uncomplicated cystitis (3-day course):**

- 81-96% cure rates
- Comparable to TMP-SMX, nitrofurantoin
- **BUT:** Not recommended first-line due to collateral damage

::: {.callout-warning}
Reserve for patients with contraindications to first-line agents
:::

::: {.notes}
While highly effective, quinolones are no longer first-line for uncomplicated UTI due to concerns about resistance selection, adverse effects, and availability of safer alternatives.
:::

## Complicated UTIs and Pyelonephritis

- 7-14 day treatment duration
- Ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin: 95% eradication rates
- Good coverage of resistant gram-negatives
- Oral bioequivalent to IV for most patients

::: {.notes}
For complicated UTIs, quinolones remain important options because they cover the resistant gram-negatives often involved and can be given orally for outpatient therapy.
:::

## Prostatitis

::: {.callout-tip}
## First-line for Chronic Bacterial Prostatitis
- Excellent prostatic penetration
- Treatment duration: 4-6 weeks
- Ciprofloxacin or levofloxacin preferred
:::

::: {.notes}
The prostatic penetration and gram-negative coverage make quinolones ideal for prostatitis. The long duration ensures eradication from the difficult-to-penetrate prostatic tissue.
:::

## Sexually Transmitted Infections

**NO longer recommended for empirical gonorrhea treatment!**

::: {.incremental}
- High resistance rates (>30% in many areas)
- Use only if susceptibility confirmed
- Still effective for *Chlamydia* and *M. genitalium*
:::

::: {.notes}
This is a major change from previous practice. Ceftriaxone is now standard for gonorrhea. However, quinolones remain useful for other STIs like chlamydia when alternatives are contraindicated.
:::

## Gastrointestinal Infections {.smaller}

| Indication | Agent | Duration |
|------------|-------|----------|
| Shigellosis | Ciprofloxacin | 3 days |
| Traveler's diarrhea | Ciprofloxacin | 1-3 days |
| Typhoid (susceptible) | Ciprofloxacin | 5-7 days |
| Severe *Salmonella* | Ciprofloxacin | 7-14 days |

::: {.callout-warning}
Watch for resistance in *Salmonella* from South/Southeast Asia
:::

::: {.notes}
Quinolones remain first-line for shigellosis and traveler's diarrhea. For typhoid, azithromycin or ceftriaxone may be preferred in areas with high quinolone resistance.
:::

## Community-Acquired Pneumonia

"Respiratory fluoroquinolones" provide:

::: {.incremental}
- Coverage of *S. pneumoniae* (including drug-resistant)
- Activity against atypical pathogens
- Single-agent monotherapy option
- Oral or IV options
:::

::: {.notes}
For patients with comorbidities, recent antibiotics, or in areas with high pneumococcal macrolide resistance, respiratory fluoroquinolones offer convenient monotherapy.
:::

## CAP: When to Use Quinolones

::: {.callout-tip}
## IDSA/ATS Guidelines
Consider respiratory fluoroquinolones for CAP in:

- Patients with comorbidities (COPD, diabetes, renal/hepatic disease)
- Recent antibiotic use (past 3 months)
- Penicillin allergy
- High local macrolide resistance
:::

::: {.notes}
Guidelines emphasize that quinolones should be reserved for specific situations rather than used as first-line for everyone. This preserves their effectiveness for patients who really need them.
:::

## Hospital-Acquired Pneumonia

- Ciprofloxacin or levofloxacin may be used
- Usually part of combination regimen
- Important for *Pseudomonas* coverage
- Consider local resistance patterns

::: {.notes}
In HAP/VAP, quinolones provide valuable antipseudomonal coverage. However, high resistance rates in many ICUs require culture-directed therapy.
:::

## Skin and Soft Tissue Infections

**Delafloxacin** — newest quinolone:

::: {.incremental}
- FDA-approved for acute bacterial SSTI
- Active against MRSA (MIC~90~ 0.5)
- Weakly acidic — enhanced activity in abscesses
- Low resistance selection potential
:::

::: {.notes}
Delafloxacin fills a niche for SSTIs where you want oral therapy but need MRSA coverage. Its acidic nature enhances activity in the low-pH environment of abscesses.
:::

## Bone and Joint Infections

Advantages of quinolones:

::: {.incremental}
- Good bone penetration
- Excellent oral bioavailability
- Allows IV → oral transition
- Activity against common pathogens
:::

::: {.callout-note}
Consider combination with rifampin for staphylococcal infections
:::

::: {.notes}
The ability to transition to oral therapy is particularly valuable for osteomyelitis requiring prolonged treatment. This allows earlier discharge and outpatient completion of therapy.
:::

## Mycobacterial Infections

**Tuberculosis:**

- Moxifloxacin, levofloxacin in MDR-TB regimens
- NOT recommended for treatment shortening in drug-sensitive TB
- Did not improve outcomes in tuberculous meningitis

**NTM:**

- Variable activity by species
- Best against *M. fortuitum*, *M. kansasii*

::: {.notes}
Despite hopes that fluoroquinolones could shorten TB treatment, clinical trials didn't support this. They remain important for drug-resistant disease.
:::

# Adverse Effects

## Overview of Safety Concerns

::: {.callout-important}
## FDA Black Box Warnings
1. Tendinitis and tendon rupture
2. Peripheral neuropathy
3. CNS effects
4. Exacerbation of myasthenia gravis
5. *C. difficile*-associated diarrhea
:::

::: {.notes}
The FDA has progressively added warnings to fluoroquinolones. These serious adverse effects have led to recommendations to reserve quinolones for situations where benefits outweigh risks.
:::

## GI Adverse Effects

Most common adverse effects (1-5%):

- Nausea
- Vomiting
- Diarrhea
- Abdominal discomfort

::: {.callout-warning}
Associated with *C. difficile* outbreaks (NAP1/BI/027 strain)
:::

::: {.notes}
GI effects are usually mild and self-limited. The C. difficile association is more concerning and has contributed to reduced quinolone use in hospitals.
:::

## CNS Adverse Effects {.smaller}

| Effect | Frequency |
|--------|-----------|
| Headache | 1-4% |
| Dizziness | 1-4% |
| Insomnia | 1-2% |
| Restlessness | 1-2% |
| **Seizures** | Rare |

Risk factors: High dose, renal impairment, concurrent NSAIDs

::: {.notes}
CNS effects are generally mild but can be troublesome, especially in elderly patients. The seizure risk is real but rare, and is enhanced by renal impairment and drug interactions.
:::

## Tendinopathy

::: {.callout-important}
## FDA Black Box Warning
- Can occur within hours to months
- Achilles tendon most common
- May be bilateral
- Can occur after stopping the drug
:::

**Risk factors:**

- Age >60 years
- Corticosteroid use
- Solid organ transplant
- Renal impairment

::: {.notes}
The tendinopathy risk has changed prescribing habits significantly. Patients should be warned to stop the drug and contact their physician if they develop tendon pain.
:::

## Peripheral Neuropathy

::: {.callout-important}
## FDA Warning
- May occur rapidly (within days)
- **May be irreversible**
- Discontinue immediately if symptoms develop
:::

Symptoms: Pain, burning, tingling, numbness, weakness

::: {.notes}
Unlike most drug-induced neuropathies, quinolone-associated neuropathy may not resolve after stopping the drug. This potential permanence is particularly concerning.
:::

## Cardiac: QT Prolongation

Relative risk by agent:

::: {.incremental}
1. **Moxifloxacin** — highest risk
2. Levofloxacin — moderate risk
3. Ciprofloxacin — lower risk
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
Avoid in: Long QT syndrome, uncorrected hypokalemia, concurrent QT-prolonging drugs
:::

::: {.notes}
This is a class effect but varies significantly by agent. If a quinolone is needed in a patient at risk, ciprofloxacin is preferred from a cardiac safety standpoint.
:::

## Dysglycemia

Both hypo- and hyperglycemia reported:

- Gatifloxacin (withdrawn from systemic use)
- Can occur without hypoglycemic agents
- Mechanism not fully understood

::: {.callout-warning}
Monitor glucose in diabetic patients
:::

::: {.notes}
Gatifloxacin was withdrawn from systemic use due to severe dysglycemia cases. Other quinolones can also affect glucose but less dramatically.
:::

## Special Populations

**Pediatrics:**

- Generally avoided (cartilage concerns)
- Ciprofloxacin approved for specific indications

**Pregnancy:**

- Category C; avoid unless necessary

**Myasthenia Gravis:**

- May exacerbate weakness — use with extreme caution

::: {.notes}
The pediatric restriction is based on animal studies showing cartilage damage in juvenile animals. However, human data hasn't consistently shown this, and quinolones are used in children when necessary.
:::

# Clinical Pearls & Summary

## When Quinolones Are Preferred

::: {.incremental}
1. Chronic bacterial prostatitis
2. Complicated UTI/pyelonephritis (resistant organisms)
3. Certain bone/joint infections (oral step-down)
4. CAP with comorbidities or recent antibiotics
5. MDR tuberculosis (moxifloxacin)
:::

::: {.notes}
These are situations where quinolone advantages clearly outweigh the risks. The excellent oral bioavailability and tissue penetration make them valuable in these specific scenarios.
:::

## When to Avoid Quinolones

::: {.incremental}
1. Uncomplicated UTI (use TMP-SMX, nitrofurantoin first)
2. Mild community-acquired infections
3. Empirical gonorrhea treatment
4. Patients with tendon disorders or prior FQ tendinopathy
5. Patients taking corticosteroids (if alternatives exist)
:::

::: {.notes}
Stewardship principles emphasize reserving quinolones for situations where they're truly needed. Using them unnecessarily contributes to resistance and exposes patients to adverse effects without clear benefit.
:::

## Key Drug Interactions to Remember

| Interaction | Management |
|-------------|------------|
| Cation-containing products | Separate by 2+ hours |
| Tizanidine | **Avoid** with ciprofloxacin |
| Theophylline | Monitor levels with ciprofloxacin |
| QT-prolonging drugs | Avoid; use ciprofloxacin if necessary |
| Warfarin | Monitor INR |

::: {.notes}
These five interactions should be committed to memory. The cation interaction is universal; the others vary by quinolone agent.
:::

## Agent Selection Summary {.smaller}

| Clinical Scenario | Preferred Agent(s) |
|-------------------|--------------------|
| Pseudomonas infection | Ciprofloxacin, Levofloxacin |
| CAP/Respiratory | Levofloxacin, Moxifloxacin |
| SSTI (including MRSA) | Delafloxacin |
| UTI/Prostatitis | Ciprofloxacin, Levofloxacin |
| Renal impairment | Moxifloxacin |
| Cardiac risk | Ciprofloxacin |
| Anaerobic coverage needed | Moxifloxacin |

::: {.notes}
This table summarizes agent selection based on clinical scenario. Matching the right quinolone to the right patient and pathogen optimizes efficacy while minimizing adverse effects.
:::

## Take-Home Points

::: {.incremental}
1. Dual topoisomerase targets, but resistance develops stepwise
2. Ciprofloxacin = best gram-negative; newer agents = better gram-positive
3. Excellent oral bioavailability enables IV-to-oral switch
4. Watch for cation interactions affecting absorption
5. Serious adverse effects limit use for mild infections
6. Reserve for appropriate indications (stewardship)
:::

::: {.notes}
These six points capture the essence of quinolone pharmacology and clinical use. Understanding these principles will help you make good prescribing decisions.
:::

## Questions?

Thank you for your attention!

::: {.callout-note}
## Key Resources
- IDSA Guidelines for various infections
- Local antibiograms for resistance patterns
- FDA Drug Safety Communications
:::

::: {.notes}
I hope this lecture has given you a solid foundation in quinolone pharmacology and clinical use. Feel free to ask questions about anything we've covered.
:::

# Appendix: Tables

## MIC Table: Gram-Negative Pathogens {.smaller}

| Organism | Cipro | Levo | Moxi |
|----------|-------|------|------|
| *E. coli* | 0.25 | 0.5 | 0.25 |
| *K. pneumoniae* | 0.5 | 0.5 | 1 |
| *P. aeruginosa* | 0.25-2 | 0.5-2 | 4-8 |
| *H. influenzae* | ≤0.03 | 0.03 | 0.03 |
| *M. catarrhalis* | ≤0.015 | ≤0.03 | ≤0.015 |

MIC~90~ values (μg/mL); resistance not shown

::: {.notes}
This slide is for reference. Note how ciprofloxacin remains the most potent agent against P. aeruginosa among available quinolones.
:::

## MIC Table: Atypical Pathogens {.smaller}

| Organism | Cipro | Levo | Moxi |
|----------|-------|------|------|
| *Legionella* spp. | 0.016-0.06 | 0.016-0.03 | 0.06 |
| *M. pneumoniae* | 0.5-4 | 0.5-2.5 | 0.12-0.3 |
| *C. pneumoniae* | 2 | 0.5-1 | 0.06-1 |
| *C. trachomatis* | 0.5-2 | 0.25-0.5 | 0.06 |

MIC values (μg/mL)

::: {.notes}
All respiratory fluoroquinolones have good activity against atypical pathogens. Moxifloxacin tends to be most potent against the chlamydial species.
:::

## Dosing Reference Card {.smaller}

| Agent | Standard Dose | Renal Adjustment |
|-------|---------------|------------------|
| Ciprofloxacin | 500 mg PO q12h | ↓ if CrCl <30 |
| Ciprofloxacin | 400 mg IV q12h | ↓ if CrCl <30 |
| Levofloxacin | 750 mg PO/IV daily | ↓ if CrCl <50 |
| Moxifloxacin | 400 mg PO/IV daily | None needed |
| Delafloxacin | 450 mg PO q12h | ↓ if CrCl <30 |

::: {.notes}
Keep this as a quick reference. Remember that moxifloxacin requires no renal adjustment, making it convenient for patients with renal impairment.
:::
